data.title: RSA Classic Encrypt / Decrypt
data.statement:
In this exercise, you will learn the basics of how RSA private and public keys are created and how they are used.

Note: Normally, you should not encrypt data letter-by-letter, but for simplicity, in this exercise you will encrypt one character at a time using a specified alphabet and decrypt one number at a time.

<<# Finding D and N>>

How are the RSA private and public keys created?

1 - Key Generation: Three large numbers are randomly chosen: [[P]], [[Q]], and [[E]]. They must be large to secure the system by making the modulus hard to factor.

2 - Public Exponent [[E]]: [[E]] is used in the modPow function (see below) for both encryption and decryption.

3 - Modulus [[N]]: [[N]] is determined by: `N = P * Q`. [[N]] serves as the modulus for the encryption and decryption of the message.

4 - Private Exponent [[D]]: Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, find [[D]] such that :
`(D * E) % ((P - 1) * (Q - 1)) = 1`

<<# Encryption and Decryption Process>>

Once you have [[D]], [[E]], and [[N]], you can begin encryption and decryption:

- {{Encryption}} uses [[E]] and [[N]].
- {{Decryption}} uses [[D]] and [[N]].

[[alphabet]] = "{{ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789 .!?-_#()[]{}@/:;,}}"

Function Requirement: Create a function modPow that returns: (base ** exponent) % modulus.

Tip: It may be impossible to calculate the result directly because base ** exponent can be an extremely large number.

<<# Encrypt>>

Inputs: [[Message]], [[N]], and [[E]]. For each character in the message:

1-Find its index in the given alphabet.
2-Encrypt this index using modPow. 

Output: Print the list of encrypted numbers as a string with {{-}} as separator, along with the variable [[D]].

<<# Decrypt>>

Inputs: Encrypted [[Message]], [[D]], and [[N]]. For each number in the encrypted string (separated by {{-}}):

1-Decrypt it using modPow.
2-Convert the resulting number back to a character using the alphabet. 

Output: Print the decrypted message and the variable [[D]].

<<# Additional explanation on how RSA key files are created is available, but it is not necessary for this exercise. >>

Once the necessary variables are determined, the tool uses ASN.1, a data description language that transforms a structure (such as those defined by X.509) into binary code using the DER encoder. This binary code is then converted to Base64, which transforms an array of bits into a string of characters.

How does ASN.1 work?
ASN.1 allows you to define your own data structures and then create data that conforms to those definitions. Key elements include :
• Field types: INTEGER, ENUMERATED, IA5String, BOOLEAN, REAL, etc.
• Optional fields or fields with value ranges
• Structures: Sequence, Sequence OF, Set OF, Choice, etc.
• Variable definitions, for example:
  age ::= INTEGER
Unfortunately, I cannot provide an exhaustive course here!

How does Base64 work?
Base64 uses an alphabet composed of 64 characters (A–Z, a–z, 0–9, plus "+" and "/"). Each character represents 6 bits. Since 2**6 = 64 bits, the encoder takes 3 bytes (i.e., 24 bits) from the input array and converts them into 4 characters from this alphabet.

Example :
 0C.57.D4 => 00001100 01010111 11010100
• 000011 . 00 0101 . 0111 11 . 010100
Output: DFfU

If there are not enough necessary bytes, the padding character {{=}} is added.
  0C = 00001100 =>
• 000011 . 00 + [0000.000000]
• 3.0 two padding
Output: DA==

  0C.57 = 00001100 01010111 =>
• 000011 . 00 0101 . 0111 [00]
• 3.5.28 with one padding 
Output: DFc=

Notes :
• ASN.1 is used in many fields (WiFi networks, HTTP(S), mobile networks, devices such as routers and servers, etc.) because it ensures uniform interpretation of data exchanged between different systems.
• Base64 is ubiquitous—for example, it is often used to encode an image within a JSON, which is very convenient.

testCases.testIn:
661
739
113
Encrypt
RSA Classic Simple Encrypt and Decrypt

testCases.title: Decrypt Hello World
testCases.testOut:
Hello World is an amazing message
2753

testCases.title: Decrypt V0
testCases.testOut:
RSA Classic Simple Encrypt and Decrypt
262937

testCases.title: Decrypt secret message !
testCases.testOut:
ASN.1 is just a way to encode data and is not a protocol
49733

testCases.title: Decrypt secret message  2 !
testCases.testOut:
ASN.1 is just a way to encode data and is not a protocol
360017

testCases.title: But what's ASN.1 ?
testCases.testOut:
OMG ! To create your own private and public key, you need to learn ASN.1, PKCS#1, PEM, PFX, DER, Base64, Hexadecimal, etc ...
49733

testCases.title: Decrypt secret mesage 3 !
testCases.testIn:
647
613
593
Encrypt
ASN.1 is just a way to encode data and is not a protocol

testCases.title: RLY ?? That's how RSA generate a private and public key ?
testCases.testIn:
311
313
317
Encrypt
OMG ! To create your own private and public key, you need to learn ASN.1, PKCS#1, PEM, PFX, DER, Base64, Hexadecimal, etc ...

read Q:int
read E:int
read Type:string(1024)
read Message:string(1024)

write EncryptDecrypt
write D
data.inputDescription:
<<Line 1 :>> [[P]] a positive integer
<<Line 2 :>> [[Q]] a positive integer
<<Line 3 :>> [[E]] a positive integer
<<Line 4 :>> [[Type]] a string which is {{Encrypt}} or {{Decrypt}}
<<Line 5 :>> [[Message]], if [[Type]] is {{Encrypt}}, the type is a string, and if it's {{Decrypt}} a list of positive integers separated by {{-}}
data.outputDescription:
<<Line 1 :>> The message in the correct format ({{Encrypt}} or {{Decrypt}} format)
<<Line 2 :>> The value [[D]]

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

In this exercise, you will learn the basics of how RSA private and public keys are created and how they are used.

Note: Normally, you should not encrypt data letter-by-letter, but for simplicity, in this exercise you will encrypt one character at a time using a specified alphabet and decrypt one number at a time.

# Finding D and N

How are the RSA private and public keys created?

1 - Key Generation: Three large numbers are randomly chosen: P, Q, and E. They must be large to secure the system by making the modulus hard to factor.

2 - Public Exponent E: E is used in the modPow function (see below) for both encryption and decryption.

3 - Modulus N: N is determined by: 
N = P * Q
. N serves as the modulus for the encryption and decryption of the message.

4 - Private Exponent D: Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, find D such that :
(D * E) % ((P - 1) * (Q - 1)) = 1


# Encryption and Decryption Process

Once you have D, E, and N, you can begin encryption and decryption:

- Encryption uses E and N.
- Decryption uses D and N.

alphabet = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789 .!?-_#()[]{}@/:;,"

Function Requirement: Create a function modPow that returns: (base ** exponent) % modulus.

Tip: It may be impossible to calculate the result directly because base ** exponent can be an extremely large number.

# Encrypt

Inputs: Message, N, and E. For each character in the message:

1-Find its index in the given alphabet.
2-Encrypt this index using modPow.

Output: Print the list of encrypted numbers as a string with - as separator, along with the variable D.

# Decrypt

Inputs: Encrypted Message, D, and N. For each number in the encrypted string (separated by -):

1-Decrypt it using modPow.
2-Convert the resulting number back to a character using the alphabet.

Output: Print the decrypted message and the variable D.

# Additional explanation on how RSA key files are created is available, but it is not necessary for this exercise.

Once the necessary variables are determined, the tool uses ASN.1, a data description language that transforms a structure (such as those defined by X.509) into binary code using the DER encoder. This binary code is then converted to Base64, which transforms an array of bits into a string of characters.

How does ASN.1 work?
ASN.1 allows you to define your own data structures and then create data that conforms to those definitions. Key elements include :
• Field types: INTEGER, ENUMERATED, IA5String, BOOLEAN, REAL, etc.
• Optional fields or fields with value ranges
• Structures: Sequence, Sequence OF, Set OF, Choice, etc.
• Variable definitions, for example:
  age ::= INTEGER
Unfortunately, I cannot provide an exhaustive course here!

How does Base64 work?
Base64 uses an alphabet composed of 64 characters (A–Z, a–z, 0–9, plus "+" and "/"). Each character represents 6 bits. Since 2**6 = 64 bits, the encoder takes 3 bytes (i.e., 24 bits) from the input array and converts them into 4 characters from this alphabet.

Example :
0C.57.D4 => 00001100 01010111 11010100
• 000011 . 00 0101 . 0111 11 . 010100
Output: DFfU

If there are not enough necessary bytes, the padding character = is added.
0C = 00001100 =>
• 000011 . 00 + [0000.000000]
• 3.0 two padding
Output: DA==

0C.57 = 00001100 01010111 =>
• 000011 . 00 0101 . 0111 [00]
• 3.5.28 with one padding
Output: DFc=

Notes :
• ASN.1 is used in many fields (WiFi networks, HTTP(S), mobile networks, devices such as routers and servers, etc.) because it ensures uniform interpretation of data exchanged between different systems.
• Base64 is ubiquitous—for example, it is often used to encode an image within a JSON, which is very convenient.
Input
Line 1 : P a positive integer
Line 2 : Q a positive integer
Line 3 : E a positive integer
Line 4 : Type a string which is Encrypt or Decrypt
Line 5 : Message, if Type is Encrypt, the type is a string, and if it's Decrypt a list of positive integers separated by -
Output
Line 1 : The message in the correct format (Encrypt or Decrypt format)
Line 2 : The value D
Constraints
P, Q, E are prime numbers.
1 < P, Q, E < 1000
Example
Input
61
53
17
Encrypt
Hello World
Output
2369-1726-1350-1350-642-2502-2558-642-706-1350-1912
2753
